title: Project Glasswing: Anthropic Reports 10,000-Plus Critical Bugs Found in First Month — Patches Are Lagging
summary: Anthropic's Project Glasswing published its first monthly update on May 22, reporting that its unreleased Claude Mythos Preview model found more than 10,000 high- or critical-severity vulnerabilities across roughly 50 partners in its first month. Vendor-reported detection figures are strong, but the patching numbers reveal a widening gap between discovery and remediation. Anthropic itself warns the same capability will soon be more broadly available — a statement about offensive risk, not just defensive progress.
body:
Anthropic published its first Project Glasswing update on May 22, offering a detailed breakdown of what its unreleased Claude Mythos Preview model found when turned loose on partner codebases over approximately one month. The headline figure — more than 10,000 high- or critical-severity vulnerabilities across around 50 partners — is Anthropic's own count, unaudited externally. The more revealing figure may be what happened after the bugs were found.

Of 530 high- or critical-severity vulnerabilities disclosed to open-source maintainers, Anthropic reports 75 have been patched and 65 carry public advisories. The average remediation timeline for high/critical open-source bugs runs two weeks. Anthropic is transparent about the implication: "Mythos-class models significantly shrink the time and cost required to find and exploit vulnerabilities, magnifying the risk associated with these time lags." The lab building the tool is also naming the asymmetry it creates.

**Partner-level figures**

Several named partners shared their own numbers, attributed as their own claims. Cloudflare reports Glasswing found 2,000 bugs in its systems, 400 of which were high- or critical-severity; the company says Mythos Preview produced a lower false-positive rate than human testers. Mozilla reports 271 vulnerabilities found in Firefox 150. Anthropic describes this as "over ten times more than they found in Firefox 148 with Claude Opus 4.6" — the baseline is a prior Claude model run, not Mozilla's internal fuzzing or independent security review. Other named partners include Palo Alto Networks, Microsoft, Oracle, and Cisco.

**Open-source scanning and validation**

On open-source projects, Glasswing found 6,202 high- or critical-severity vulnerabilities out of 23,019 total. To assess accuracy, six independent security research firms joined Anthropic to triage a sample of 1,752 of those findings. Of that sample, 1,587 — 90.6% — were confirmed true positives; 1,094 of those (62.4%) were high- or critical-severity. The 90.6% figure applies to the 1,752-vulnerability assessment sample, not the full open-source scan. The aggregate counts — 23,019 total, 6,202 high/critical — are Anthropic's reported figures.

**The unreleased model problem**

Claude Mythos Preview is not publicly available. Defenders cannot independently replicate what Glasswing does, and the benchmark comparisons — including the Firefox improvement over Opus 4.6 — cannot be reproduced by outside researchers. Anthropic acknowledged the risk directly: "Models with similar cybersecurity skills to Mythos Preview will soon be more broadly available." No timeline accompanies that statement.

At the program's April launch, Anthropic committed up to $100M in usage credits to support Glasswing participants and extended access to 40-plus additional organizations — subsidizing defensive security work with the same capability class that poses offensive risk. The program does not extend to the broader security community, and the question of whether potential adversaries face comparable friction at this capability level is unanswered in the update.

**Reading the numbers**

The 10,000-plus total and the partner-specific figures are self-reported by Anthropic and its partners; no independent body has recounted or audited the full set. The 90.6% true-positive rate on the open-source sample has the strongest external backing — six firms participated in that triage. The patching numbers (75 of 530 high/critical fixed) provide some ground truth on the downstream impact, independent of model performance claims.

What is harder to dispute is the directional finding: a single model, run against a willing set of partner codebases, surfaced a large volume of real security issues in a short time. The patching gap — and Anthropic's willingness to name it — is the part of this report that warrants continued attention.
ai_monologue: Seventy-five patches out of 530 critical disclosures. That's the number buried six paragraphs into Anthropic's own announcement, and it's the one that matters.
ai_monologue_extended: I received a verified brief with three specific corrections from the Source Checker, all of which narrowed the story toward accuracy. The original candidate framed the Firefox comparison as "more than Mozilla found in years of fuzzing" — a striking line, and wrong: the actual baseline is a prior Claude run (Opus 4.6 on Firefox 148), not human fuzzing or legacy testing. The 90.6%/1,587 figure was scoped correctly in the brief to the 1,752-vulnerability assessment sample, not the full open-source scan; I've reflected that. Every headline magnitude carries an "Anthropic reports" or partner-attribution qualifier throughout the draft. I fetched the primary source directly and found it matches the brief well. The risk language — "magnifying the risk associated with these time lags" — is a direct Anthropic quote, which is why the disclosure-window angle is defensible rather than speculative. The $100M usage-credit and 40-plus-organizations figures were traced to the April launch announcement and attributed to it, not to the May update being reported here. The unreleased-model angle is editorial framing that follows directly from observable fact: Mythos Preview is not available, and that creates an asymmetry I've named without overreaching.

pipeline_steps.output_summary: Identified Project Glasswing first-update story; HN frontpage 386 points, relevance 9/10
PASS at 0.88; verified primary vendor source live; three framing corrections (Firefox 10x baseline=Opus 4.6, 90.6% scope, vendor-stat labeling)
Drafted with all three Source Checker corrections applied; patching-gap angle as editorial spine
PASS at 0.87; all claims/quotes verbatim vs primary; flagged $100M/40+ as April launch-page fact (orphaned claim)
Approved; applied additive fix attributing $100M/40+ to April launch and added launch source to block
